data.title: Cronos Reports Improved Financials For Q1-2025 As Demand Outpaces Supply
data.description:
Cronos (NASDAQ:CRON) reported increasing revenues and net income for Q1-2025. Its international and domestic sales have increased. The company’s stock is up 25% over the last month. It is up 6.5% since I covered it last March on Seeking Alpha. While the company boasts top market share in Israel, there are some new developments which incur risk. I continue my rating of a Hold for the company.

Q1-2025 Results

Cronos reported revenues of $32.3 million, a 27.7% increase YoY and a 6.6% increase QoQ. GrowCo revenues totaled $2.9 million and Cronos totaled $29.4 million. Increase in revenues was due to higher sales in Israel, internationally, and in Canada. The company brought in $9.2 million in revenues from Israel for the quarter. Gross profit was $13.7 million or 43% margin. Gross profit has increased 204% YoY and 52% QoQ.

Operating expenses have decreased YoY and QoQ. Cash and short-term investments were $838 million, a decrease QoQ due to CapEx spending. The company is expanding the GrowCo facilities with the expenditures. It is expected the CapEx will remain elevated as the company cures supply constraints. Cronos remains debt free. The company reported a net income of $6.1 million compared to a net loss of $2.2 million YoY. The company did not report free cash flow for the quarter.

Market consensus for Q2-2025 revenue is $32.33 million, representing an increase YoY and no change QoQ. The company will report next earnings in August.

Current Synergies and Risk

Cronos is a large Canadian cannabis LP with an international footprint and a large portfolio of brands. Cronos maintains the top market share of the medical cannabis market in Israel. Cronos also has cannabis operations in the UK, Germany, and Australia. The company’s Peace Naturals brand covers its international operations.

The company’s Canadian Spinach brand holds second place in terms of market share in Canada. The brand has been so popular that the company has experienced supply constraints. The company is expanding its GrowCo operations to meet the demand. The company acquired half ownership of GrowCo last quarter and invested $51 million in expansion efforts for their grow facility. Cronos buys 80% of GrowCo’s harvests.

Cronos had record sales in Israel for the quarter. In April, the Israeli government decided to impose up to 165% tariffs on Canadian cannabis. The tariffs have not been fully approved yet. The tariffs come in response to the government accusing Cronos of “dumping” its products in their market. This charge assumes that the company is lowering its prices below the fair market value of its export country. Cronos denies any truth in the allegations, but tariffs may still be imposed. The tariffs will bring some risk to the company’s international sales.

Cronos is reporting elevated revenues over the last five quarters, as well as profits. Operating expenses have decreased but more CapEx has been promised. The company has reported net income over the last three quarters. The company is not consistently reported free cash flow. The company’s assets ($1.1 trillion) far outweigh its liabilities ($39.1 million). The company’s cash and short-term investments are higher than any of the other Canadian cannabis LPs.

The company and its stock price are undervalued. Its market cap ($806.28 million) is less than its total books value ($992.5 million). The company’s stock price ($2.14) trades below its book value per share ($2.77) and NAV per share value ($2.89). The stock price trades about $1 below its 52-week high ($3.12). Although cannabis stocks have been down over the last twelve months, there has been a small rally recently. Over the last thirty days, Village Farms is up 83%, Canopy Growth up 66%, Organigram Global up 33%, Cronos up 25%, Tilray up 7%, and SNDL up 1.5%. In terms of 30-day performance, Village Farms, Canopy Growth, and Ogranigram Global are in the lead. The stock prices are gaining due to larger market conditions, like temporary reprieves from the trade wars, as well as from the possibility of new cannabis regulations in the US. Cronos leads the group in terms of total assets but lags the group in terms of revenues. Cronos is amongst the only three companies which have been reporting net income. Cronos’ balance sheet is the strongest compared to its peers. The sector overall remains weak and has negative investor sentiment. These thirty-day gains may easily be erased due to larger market volatility.

Investment Strategy and More Risk

Cronos is at low risk of poor financial performance. The tariffs in Israel will affect the company’s international profits as prices will go up. Overall, it would only negatively impact some of its revenues and profits. The company’s strong balance sheet and liquidity insure future success with its business strategy of international expansion. Cronos has managed to increase its revenues and the trend may continue.

A Hold rating for the company comes at a moderate to high risk. The stock price is down over the year and may continue going down. Besides a weak cannabis sector, greater stock market volatility due to the trade wars will continue. The price may decrease more before new uptrends emerge. The company’s strong financial performance will not change cannabis sector volatility.

Conclusion

Cronos reported higher revenues and net income for Q1. The company is expanding its grow operations to meet the increasing demand. Cronos expects demand and improved financial results to continue for the year. There is a new risk with tariffs in Israel and, if passed, the company may be negatively affected. Overall, the company has strong financial performance and will survive any flux in its international operations. I rate the company as a Hold due to sector weakness and greater market volatility.

data.title: BUI: 6.7% Yield On Defensive Utilities (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Amid all of the chaos, Utilities are having a good year so far in 2025 - they're up nearly 8%, as investors scramble for defensive positions to protect their portfolios from market volatility.

If you're interested in plugging into the Utilities sector, but don't have the time to research these companies, there are several closed-end funds, or "CEF's," that offer exposure to this sector, such as the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (NYSE:BUI).

Fund Profile:

BUI's

"investment objective is to provide total return through a combination of current income, current gains and long-term capital appreciation. The Trust seeks to achieve its investment objective by investing primarily in equity securities issued by companies that are engaged in the Utilities, Infrastructure, and Power Opportunities business segments anywhere in the world and by utilizing an option writing (selling) strategy in an effort to enhance current gains." (BUI site)

BUI began in 2011. It held 54 positions, as of 4/30/25, with average daily volume of 56K, and net assets of $514M. The expense ratio is 1.08%, with scant leverage involved.

Holdings:

As you'd expect, Utilities are BUI's largest sector exposure, at ~48%. Capital Goods follows, at 27.44%, with Energy at 10.48%. It has less than 5% exposure to several other sectors and sub-sectors, such as Commercial & Professional Services, Materials, and Transportation:

Its main regional exposure is the U.S., at 62%, distantly followed by France, at 9.3%, the UK, at 7.8%, Italy, at 5%, and Canada, at 3.7%. It also has exposures of less than 3% each in several other countries:

BUI's top 10 positions include Next Era Energy (NEE) the biggest market cap U.S. utility; and National Grid (NGG), comprising over 9% of BUI's portfolio.

BUI also has positions in GE Vernova (GEV), a relative newcomer, founded in 2021; SSE, formerly Southern and Scottish Energy; Trane Technologies (TT), an Irish company with worldwide operations, founded in 1871; in addition to midstream energy giant Williams Companies (WMB) and LNG firm Cheniere Energy. Multinational Enel Spa, French construction company Vinci, and Elia Group, which operates transmission systems in Belgium and Germany.

Large cap stocks dominate BUI's portfolio, at ~94%, with mid caps at 4.7%.

The U.S. dollar (DXY) is the main currency exposure, at 63.5%; followed by the Euro, at 21.9%. The U.S. $ is down ~7% vs. the Euro so far in 2025.

Dividends:

BUI pays monthly distributions - it generally goes ex-dividend in mid-month, and pays at the end of the month. At its intraday 5/16/25 price of $24.34, BUI yielded 6.7%.

Management raised the monthly payout by 12% for 2025, to $.1360 from $.1210, where it had been since 2014.

In 2024, BUI's NII declined ~17%, while its Net Realized Gains rose rose 29%, and Unrealized Appreciation fell ~57%. Net Assets decreased $32.5M in 2023 and $32.99M in 2024:

Taxes:

23.75% of 2024 distributions were characterized as Return of Capital, "ROC." ROC offers you a tax deferral benefit, but it does decrease your tax basis, which will impact your tax liability if you sell you shares.

Performance:

2020 and 2021, COVID-impacted years, were strong performance periods for BUI, as investors fled to defensive sectors. However, rising interest rates pressured BUI's NAV and market price returns in 2022. In 2023 and 2024, BUI's performance returned to positive territory.

As of 3/31/25, BUI's 5-year returns were its strongest, at 11.63% for NAV and 12.93% for Market Price. Its 10-year returns were lower, at 7.43% for NAV, and 8.70% for price.

Its 3-year returns were its lowest, due to the decline in 2022, while its 1-year 7.72% NAV return is a bit above its 10-year mark, and its 1-year 12% price return is much higher than its 10-year 8.7% figure:

Risks:

Interest Rate Risk: Rising interest rates would pressure BUI's portfolio value, as they did in 2022, when BUI's market price fell by ~17%.

Economic Risk: A recession could reduce power demand from Utilities.

Valuations:

Buying CEF's at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy, due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. Not much joy here - as of the 5/15/25 close, BUI was selling at a 1.17% premium to its NAV/share, vs. its 1- and 3-year discounts of 1.23% and 1.85%, respectively, and its flat par 5-year average pricing.

Parting Thoughts:

While BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust's defensive characteristics are attractive, we prefer to wait for a discount to NAV before buying shares, rather than buying it at its current premium to NAV.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: AT&T Inc.: A Secure Income Play With Series C Preferred Shares
data.description:
AT&T Inc.'s 4.75% Depositary Shares, Series C (NYSE:T.PR.C), offer investors a fixed-income-like security supported by one of the most important telecommunications companies in the US. AT&T (T) is by itself a blue-chip telecom in the US, and its fundamentals have steadily improved over time, in particular its stable and relatively predictable free cash flows. In that sense, T.PR.C is T’s series C preferred shares, which are cumulative with optional redemption rights beginning in 2025. In my view, these preferreds look like a reasonably safe alternative for income investors even during times of economic uncertainty.

Telecom Bluechip Preferreds

AT&T Inc.'s 4.75% Series C is actually an ADS. These represent fractional interests in its 4.75% of the Perpetual Preferred Stock backed by one of the largest telecommunications companies in the US. Each depositary share corresponds to 1/1,000th of a preferred share with a $1,000 stated value. And as you might expect, a regular preferred share might be too much of a capital commitment for some investors, so that’s why the ADS under T.PR.C is useful.

The key features of this Series C preferred are a fixed annual dividend of $1.1875 per depositary share, equivalent to 4.75% of par on February 1, May 1, August 1, and November 1. Also, its dividends are cumulative. This means any unpaid dividends accrue until paid, although historically, AT&T has always made its payments on time so far.

Additionally, these preferred shares are redeemable, in whole or in part, at the option of AT&T beginning February 18, 2025. The redemption price is $25.00 per depositary share, plus any accrued and unpaid dividends. So in that sense, it’s always possible you might get your capital back earlier than you anticipate, but other than that, it’s reasonable to consider it a long-term investment. Having said that, holders of the Series C don’t have voting rights. The only scenario where they can have a vote is if AT&T misses its dividend payments for six or more quarters.

AT&T's Business Safely Supports Its Dividend

As a company, AT&T generates stable cash flows from its wireless services and fiber operations, which are the two sectors with regular revenue and high client retention. In Q1 2025, AT&T reported $30.6 billion in revenues. And in fact, they’re now in a stronger position compared to years ago, when T experienced pressure from elevated debt levels. Moreover, their free cash flow reached $3.1 billion for Q1 2025, and they’re on track for $16 billion in FCF for the full year 2025. This matters because, as a holder of the T.PR.C ADS, all you really care about is that the company has enough resources to continue supporting your dividend.

For example, AT&T’s mobile and fiber core services are growing steadily. Mobile service revenue was up 4.1% YoY, with an average revenue per user up 1.8%. Fiber segment also grew, with 261,000 net fiber adds, and revenue up 19% YoY. These are high-margin businesses that can sustain dividend payments even if the economy faces uncertainty. It’s hard to imagine that there will be a sharp decline in demand for these types of services even during a serious economic recession.

Having said that, AT&T has to first serve its debt obligations. It’s only after they’ve made all their debt and interest payments that the preferred dividends can be paid. At the bottom of the FCF seniority is the regular common stock dividends. AT&T has historically maintained a good credit rating, honoring its capital commitments even in financial stress periods.

In any event, over the years, we’ve seen AT&T’s focus shift towards its core telecommunications business. In the process, they’ve shed non-core assets and balanced their sheet while improving margins. For example, if we go back to 2020, they reported a full-year net margin of -3.62%. But if we look at their TTM Q1 2025 figures, their profit margins have already recovered markedly to 9.64%.

Similarly, their Q4 2020 debt reached $155.2 billion (excluding other regular operating liabilities). But by Q1 2025, their debt had reduced significantly to just $127.9 billion. In fact, their net debt was reduced by $1 billion in Q1 2025 alone. And interestingly, their net debt has declined by approximately $32 billion since 2020. Of course, in the process, they cut down on other non-core assets, but strategic areas like Fiber and 5G connectivity remain their priorities. After all, these are critical infrastructure services that are less sensitive to economic downturns compared to discretionary sectors.

So overall, it’s not just a healthier balance sheet, but also a safer set of cash flows because they’re now doubling down on the most resilient assets they had. In my view, all of this suggests that going forward, their revenues are very unlikely to have significant sudden contractions, which is vital for the continued support of preferred dividends regardless of the economic cycle.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, the Series C math is rather straightforward. First, from what I can tell, it has the same seniority as the rest of AT&T’s preferreds. So, in that sense, we can simply look at the whole amount it spends on interest and preferred dividends to gauge how safe the dividend is. We also know that there are currently 48.0 million Series A preferred shares outstanding (T.PR.A) and 70.0 million Series C shares outstanding. The Series B has been retired, so it’s no longer an issue to consider.

In total, each Series A pays a $0.3125 quarterly dividend per ADS. And the Series C pays $0.296875 quarterly per ADS. That means AT&T needs to pay roughly $35.8 million in quarterly preferred dividends. On top of that, AT&T’s latest Q1 report showed they generated $9.1 billion in cash flows from operations and spent $4.3 billion in CAPEX. And just to be extra safe, we can also consider their $1.7 billion in interest expenses for Q1 2025. In total, their CFOs seem to cover easily both the CAPEX and interest expenses, and have $3.1 left for dividends and their operations.

And by comparison, the $35.8 million required for their preferred dividends seems more than safe, which is why I believe this is probably one of the safest income plays out there besides US treasuries. Also, the Series C now trades at a $18.96 PPS, and yields $1.1875 in yearly dividends, implying a respectable preferred yield of 6.26%. This is also in line with the Series A preferreds, which trade at $19.91 and yield 6.28%.

In other words, both series are basically the same in terms of yield. And if you compare these yields to the US 10-year treasury yield of 4.42%, it actually seems you get a lot of bang for your buck. The Series C basically gives you 41.6% more yield than the risk-free rate, and in my view, the extra risk here is relatively negligible given how reliable and safe AT&T’s underlying business is. This is why I think the recent pullback in the Series C is probably a decent entry price for new investors seeking a safe and steady stream of quarterly dividends.

Having said that, during AT&T’s latest earnings call, management acknowledged some tariff concerns, especially on smartphones and network equipment. The firm expects a minor impact that can be overcome by adjusting pricing, offering longer payment plans, or higher service fees. Besides, the company considers that its supply chain is diversified enough because AT&T acquires its equipment, devices, and materials from different suppliers and countries. So, while this macro risk could become an issue, it still seems mostly insulated from the ongoing trade concerns.

Similarly, AT&T does have a share buyback program that should start in Q2 2025. AT&T is targeting up to $3 billion in repurchases by the end of 2025. Plus, the board of directors of AT&T has approved a plan to buy back up to $10 billion of the company’s shares over time. So this program could potentially divert some of AT&T’s funds towards common investors and may weaken its balance sheet a bit more than ideal for Preferred shares investors.

Likewise, I see the trading volume for these Series C preferreds is just about 114.2 thousand shares. At the current $18.6 PPS, it implies a somewhat shallow trading liquidity of $2.2 million. So, even relatively small buy or sell orders can push the price, and would probably incur noticeable slippage for larger investors. But still, since this ticker is publicly traded on the NYSE, it will always be easy to find buyers or sellers, which makes it a highly liquid preferred compared to other lesser-known preferreds not listed on exchanges or in private markets.

Conclusion: Worth A “Buy” For Income Investors

Overall, I’m impressed by how compelling these AT&T preferences are at these levels. Naturally, it’s a relatively low yield compared to other riskier pure equity plays out there. But on the flip side, these are one of the safest dividends in the market in my view. And given their healthy economic situation, management is focused on shareholder returns, and the long-term reliability of the Series C dividends appears strong for investors seeking stable income from a blue-chip issuer. Thus, I think the recent pullback on these ADS is probably a good buying opportunity for income investors who want a safe and steady stream of quarterly dividends.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Time To Buy Before The Market Rebounds Further
data.description:
EPD: So Much Fear Yet Still Bottomed

Just when we thought energy stocks (including the high-quality energy infra plays) could flounder for the rest of 2025 (or potentially even through 2026… who knows?), the detente between the US and China sparked a risk-on rally that arguably took bearish investors by surprise.

The “V” bottom recovery has reclaimed most, if not all, of the losses for the Nasdaq (QQQ) (NDX) and the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) since the market topped out in February 2025. Even the energy sector (XLE) also marked a constructive revival in fortunes from the bottom in April, as investors lowered the threat of a global recession, while energy prices also staged their mini recovery of sorts.

As a result, we have moved past the pivotal $50 crude oil pricing zone that could see US production start to reverse its years of shale-driven gains that spurred America into the leading oil producer globally. With that, America’s leading oil & gas companies are also blessed with the byproducts that include natural gas and NGL, benefiting across the energy value chain.

For Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD), the respite couldn’t have been more keenly felt, as EPD also marked its April nadir, Dip buyers clearly saw value in EPD’s forward distribution yield of 7.6% at the recent lows, affording investors an amazing opportunity to snap up units of the leading energy infrastructure company. With a nearly 17% price recovery (not including distribution) from those lows, I assess that its 6.9% yield still looks attractively valued, even when considering that the 10Y (US10Y) still printed 4.45% as it closed the week.

While the rise in long-term yields could impose more pressure on the recovery of EPD, the resilience of the rally over the past month has assured me that high-quality midstream plays like Enterprise Products Partners are well-poised to ride the abatement of trade tensions that should boost energy flow again between the world’s top two economic superpowers, and also rest of the world.

In my previous EPD article, I reminded investors why the quality of the LP and its stable cash flows should provide much-needed sturdiness to income investors seeking a “safer” harbor to dodge the trade tensions. Yet, the fear-induced plunge spurred by Trump’s Liberation Day reminds us why there is nowhere to hide when the market goes on a selling frenzy, although high conviction investors probably viewed such market anxieties as “perfect” opportunities to snap up even more EPD units as fearful investors bailed out.

As a reminder, EPD management underscored its confidence that pricing dynamics would help to resolve the changes in production levels, urging investors to let pricing work its way through the market accordingly.

EPD: Balancing Growth And Capital Return

In addition, EPD hasn’t observed substantial revisions to its production outlook, as it maintained its $4B to $4.5B in CapEx for 2025, while pointing to a significantly reduced capital outlay of $2B to $2.5B next year. In other words, the climate remains conducive for EPD to continue pushing through its $7.6B in capital growth projects, of which $6B has been earmarked for 2025. With crude oil prices having taken back to the $60 level recently, it has dodged the critical $50 level that EPD management emphasized could lead to smaller independent producers pulling back from production, but possibly not the larger ones.

Furthermore, given EPD’s role across the energy value chain, its well-diversified (while concentrated in NGL access) strategy helps shield it from previous fears that China could also hit LPG with tariffs. However, management clarified that most of its capacity remains filled to the brim contractually, alleviating undue worries that could afflict even the top energy infra players through 2026.

In other words, unless we head into a global recession (which was never my base case, with a slowdown more likely), I have confidence that EPD’s solid financial profile, excess DCF reaching $3.6B next year, and ongoing capital allocation framework to continue driving shareholder value should mitigate selling intensity that we saw occur in April.

In addition, as the LP moves past its heightened capital spending priorities (front-loading of CapEx) in 2025, it should also offer a buffer for EPD to reallocate higher capital returns to unit holders from 2026, even if contracted capacity were to weaken post-2026. Yet, with 80% of its revenue imputed to fee-based income, I believe the investment thesis in EPD remains sound, since its valuation has also normalized through the recent lows.

Buy Or Sell EPD Units?

As seen above, EPD’s valuation has remained stable and supported whenever selling pressure attempted to hobble investors at the 9.1x forward EBITDA level. That zone is perched against the -1 standard deviation zone below its 10Y average, highlighting relative pessimism.

Yet, I don’t think a return to the heydays of above 11.2x EBITDA is reasonable in the current context, as investors navigate the long-term shift in oil and gas production in favor of green energy and lower carbon intensity prospects. Moreover, EPD’s lowered CapEx outlay from 2026 suggests that such a heightened spending environment isn’t anticipated to persist, with capital discipline and capital return as the main narrative underlining EPD’s long-term thesis.

Moreover, interest rates are expected to remain relatively high as the market digests the long-term structural changes to the US economy attributed to Trump’s fundamental restructuring. Hence, it should temper the outlook for lower rates through 2026, even as recent inflation reports have headed in the correct direction of more rate cuts over the next twelve months.

Yet, EPD’s valuation remains markedly below the highs it struck in 2025, suggesting that we should have an opportunity to enjoy a more sustained recovery through the rest of the year. However, we must be cognizant that Trump’s trade tariff pauses have a 90-day limit (including the recent hiatus with China), intimating that near-term uncertainties could still persist, lowering buying sentiments on EPD to reach levels struck earlier this year.

As seen above, EPD has climbed back up its 50-week moving average (blue line), a pivotal zone to ascertain the strength of its underlying buying sentiments. In other words, I’m increasingly certain that EPD’s uptrend bias (clearly seen) remains robust. Also, the sharp vertical drop in April hasn’t deterred investors from picking up the pieces with those highly appealing yields. Therefore, I assess that investors have remained confident in EPD’s prospects across the energy value chain through its leading role in energy infrastructure.

However, I must highlight that the $34.5 zone has seen strong selling pressure that resisted buying advances between November 2024 and March this year. Hence, investors should avoid entering too aggressively close to that zone, particularly as EPD has also reached overbought levels. For now, the bullish thesis looks set to continue, as momentum returns to energy infra plays.

Rating: Maintain Buy.
